quiz_questions.question_text: Coronary artery disease is caused primarily by:

options.text: Genetics alone
Atherosclerosis (plaque buildup) in the coronary arteries
Valve disease only
Viral infection of the heart

quiz_questions.explanation: Coronary artery disease develops when atherosclerotic plaques composed of cholesterol, fat, calcium, and inflammatory cells accumulate in the coronary artery walls, progressively narrowing the lumen and restricting blood flow to the myocardium.

quiz_questions.question_text: A myocardial infarction (heart attack) occurs when:

options.text: The heart beats too slowly
A coronary artery is blocked, causing death of heart muscle tissue
Heart valves fail to close properly
Blood pressure drops suddenly

quiz_questions.explanation: A myocardial infarction occurs when a coronary artery becomes completely occluded, usually by a blood clot forming on a ruptured atherosclerotic plaque, cutting off oxygen supply and causing irreversible death (necrosis) of the downstream heart muscle.

quiz_questions.question_text: Symptoms of a myocardial infarction typically include:

options.text: Only mild fatigue
Chest pain or pressure, shortness of breath, sweating, nausea, and pain radiating to the arm or jaw
Only a headache
Only leg swelling

quiz_questions.explanation: Classic MI symptoms include substernal chest pain or pressure, diaphoresis, dyspnea, nausea, and radiating pain to the left arm or jaw, though presentations can be atypical, especially in women, elderly patients, and diabetics.

quiz_questions.question_text: Heart failure means the heart:

options.text: Has stopped beating completely
Cannot pump efficiently enough to meet the body's needs
Has an arrhythmia
Has a valve that is too large

quiz_questions.explanation: Heart failure is a chronic condition in which the weakened or stiffened heart cannot pump blood effectively enough to meet the body's metabolic demands, leading to fluid congestion in the lungs and/or peripheral tissues.

quiz_questions.question_text: Angina pectoris is:

options.text: A heart attack causing permanent muscle death
Chest pain from temporary myocardial ischemia without tissue death
A type of heart valve disease
An infection of the heart lining

quiz_questions.explanation: Angina pectoris is chest pain caused by temporary, reversible ischemia of the heart muscle (reduced blood flow but no cell death), often triggered by exertion or stress and relieved by rest or nitroglycerin, serving as a warning sign of underlying coronary artery disease.

quiz_questions.question_text: Risk factors for coronary artery disease include:

options.text: Only genetics
Hypertension, high cholesterol, smoking, diabetes, obesity, and family history
Only advanced age
Only a sedentary lifestyle

quiz_questions.explanation: CAD is a multifactorial disease with both modifiable risk factors (hypertension, high LDL cholesterol, smoking, diabetes, obesity, sedentary lifestyle) and non-modifiable risk factors (age, sex, family history), requiring comprehensive risk management.

quiz_questions.question_text: LDL cholesterol is called 'bad' cholesterol because:

options.text: It removes cholesterol from arteries
High LDL deposits cholesterol in artery walls, contributing to plaque buildup
It is produced only by eating saturated fat
It destroys red blood cells

quiz_questions.explanation: LDL (low-density lipoprotein) carries cholesterol from the liver to the tissues; elevated LDL promotes cholesterol deposition in the arterial intima, triggering an inflammatory response that leads to atherosclerotic plaque formation.

quiz_questions.question_text: HDL cholesterol is called 'good' cholesterol because:

options.text: It deposits cholesterol in arteries
It helps remove cholesterol from arteries and transport it back to the liver (reverse cholesterol transport)
It has no function in the body
It increases blood clotting

quiz_questions.explanation: HDL (high-density lipoprotein) is cardioprotective because it picks up excess cholesterol from arterial walls and transports it back to the liver for excretion (reverse cholesterol transport), effectively reducing atherosclerotic plaque burden.

quiz_questions.question_text: Peripheral artery disease primarily causes:

options.text: Arm swelling and numbness
Leg pain with walking (claudication) due to reduced arterial blood flow
Headaches and blurred vision
Chest pain at rest

quiz_questions.explanation: Peripheral artery disease involves atherosclerosis of the arteries supplying the extremities, most commonly the legs, causing intermittent claudication (cramping leg pain during walking) that resolves with rest because the narrowed arteries cannot meet increased oxygen demand.

quiz_questions.question_text: Cardiovascular disease is the _____ cause of death worldwide.

options.text: Least common
Fifth leading
Leading (number one)
Tenth leading

quiz_questions.explanation: Cardiovascular disease, including coronary artery disease, stroke, and heart failure, is the leading cause of death globally, accounting for approximately 17.9 million deaths per year according to the World Health Organization.

quiz_questions.question_text: What is valvular stenosis?

options.text: A valve that leaks and allows blood to flow backward
Narrowing of a heart valve opening that restricts blood flow through it
A normal variation in valve size
Complete absence of a heart valve

quiz_questions.explanation: Valvular stenosis occurs when a heart valve becomes stiffened, thickened, or calcified, narrowing the opening and restricting blood flow, which forces the upstream chamber to work harder to push blood through the constricted orifice.

quiz_questions.question_text: What is valvular regurgitation?

options.text: A blocked valve that prevents all blood flow
A leaking valve that allows blood to flow backward through it when closed
A normal finding in healthy hearts
Excessive calcium deposits on the valve

quiz_questions.explanation: Valvular regurgitation (insufficiency) occurs when a valve fails to close completely, allowing blood to leak backward; this reduces forward cardiac output and increases the workload on the affected chamber, potentially leading to heart failure.

quiz_questions.question_text: What is an aortic aneurysm?

options.text: A blood clot in the aorta
A dangerous bulging of a weakened aortic wall that can rupture, causing life-threatening hemorrhage
A narrowing of the aortic valve
A normal widening of the aorta with age

quiz_questions.explanation: An aortic aneurysm is a pathological dilation of the aortic wall caused by degenerative weakening, and it poses a serious risk of rupture, especially when it exceeds 5.5 cm in the abdominal aorta, which would cause massive internal bleeding.

quiz_questions.question_text: What is cardiac catheterization?

options.text: A blood pressure measurement technique
A procedure that threads a catheter through blood vessels to the heart to visualize blockages and allow interventions like angioplasty and stent placement
A non-invasive ultrasound of the heart
An ECG recording technique

quiz_questions.explanation: Cardiac catheterization is both a diagnostic and therapeutic procedure where a thin catheter is inserted into a peripheral artery and guided to the heart to inject contrast dye for imaging coronary arteries and, if needed, perform balloon angioplasty and stent placement.

quiz_questions.question_text: What does 'statin drug' mean?

options.text: A blood thinner that prevents clots
A medication that inhibits HMG-CoA reductase to reduce LDL cholesterol production in the liver
A drug that lowers blood pressure by blocking calcium channels
A drug that increases heart rate

quiz_questions.explanation: Statins are a class of drugs that inhibit the enzyme HMG-CoA reductase in the liver, which is critical for cholesterol synthesis, thereby significantly lowering LDL cholesterol levels and reducing the risk of atherosclerotic cardiovascular events.

quiz_questions.question_text: What is a pacemaker (artificial)?

options.text: A medication that controls heart rhythm
An implanted electronic device that delivers electrical impulses to regulate heart rhythm, primarily used for bradycardia
A surgical procedure to replace heart valves
A type of external defibrillator

quiz_questions.explanation: An artificial pacemaker is a small battery-powered device implanted under the skin with leads attached to the heart that delivers precisely timed electrical impulses to maintain an adequate heart rate when the natural conduction system fails.

quiz_questions.question_text: What is myocardial ischemia?

options.text: Death of heart muscle from prolonged blockage
Reduced blood flow to the heart muscle causing oxygen deprivation without immediate cell death
Inflammation of the pericardium
Excessive blood supply to the heart

quiz_questions.explanation: Myocardial ischemia is a reversible condition in which reduced coronary blood flow deprives the heart muscle of adequate oxygen, causing chest pain (angina) but not permanent tissue death; if prolonged, ischemia progresses to infarction.

quiz_questions.question_text: What is reperfusion therapy?

options.text: A therapy that permanently increases heart rate
Treatment to restore blood flow to ischemic tissue, including angioplasty/stenting or thrombolytic drugs to dissolve clots
A type of heart transplant procedure
A therapy that reduces blood volume

quiz_questions.explanation: Reperfusion therapy aims to quickly restore blood flow to ischemic heart muscle during a myocardial infarction, either mechanically through percutaneous coronary intervention (angioplasty/stenting) or pharmacologically with thrombolytic drugs that dissolve the occluding clot.

quiz_questions.question_text: What does 'edema' mean in the context of heart failure?

options.text: Increased blood cell production
Swelling caused by excess fluid accumulating in tissues due to the heart's inability to pump blood effectively
A type of blood clot
A heart rhythm abnormality

quiz_questions.explanation: In heart failure, the weakened heart cannot adequately circulate blood, causing blood to back up and increase hydrostatic pressure in the capillaries, forcing fluid out into the surrounding tissues and causing swelling (edema), particularly in the ankles, legs, and lungs.

quiz_questions.question_text: What is coronary artery bypass grafting (CABG)?

options.text: A catheter-based procedure to place a stent
An open-heart surgery that uses a vessel graft to create a new pathway around blocked coronary arteries
A medication that dissolves coronary artery plaque
A type of heart transplant

quiz_questions.explanation: CABG is a major surgical procedure in which a healthy blood vessel (often from the leg or chest wall) is grafted to bypass a severely blocked coronary artery, restoring blood flow to the ischemic myocardium downstream of the blockage.

quiz_questions.question_text: A 62-year-old smoker with diabetes and hypertension suddenly develops crushing chest pain, sweating, and shortness of breath. Paramedics administer aspirin immediately. Why is aspirin given as a first-line treatment in a suspected MI?

options.text: Aspirin reduces chest pain by acting as an analgesic
Aspirin inhibits platelet aggregation, helping to prevent the clot from growing larger and improving blood flow to the ischemic heart muscle
Aspirin lowers blood pressure rapidly
Aspirin dissolves the existing blood clot

quiz_questions.explanation: Aspirin irreversibly inhibits cyclooxygenase in platelets, preventing the formation of thromboxane A2 and reducing platelet aggregation; this limits the growth of the coronary thrombus and improves blood flow past the partially occluded artery.

quiz_questions.question_text: A 70-year-old woman with a history of heart failure wakes up at 3 AM gasping for air and must sit upright to breathe. This symptom is called paroxysmal nocturnal dyspnea. What causes it?

options.text: Anxiety causing hyperventilation during sleep
When lying flat, fluid redistributes from the legs to the lungs, overwhelming the weakened left ventricle and causing pulmonary congestion
A blood clot traveled to the lungs during sleep
Sleep apnea blocking the airway

quiz_questions.explanation: In left-sided heart failure, lying flat causes redistribution of peripheral edema fluid back into the central circulation; the failing left ventricle cannot handle this increased venous return, causing blood to back up into the pulmonary vasculature and fluid to leak into the alveoli, producing acute dyspnea.

quiz_questions.question_text: A patient with severe aortic stenosis develops left ventricular hypertrophy visible on an echocardiogram. The cardiologist explains this is a compensatory response. Why does the left ventricle thicken in response to aortic stenosis?

options.text: The ventricle enlarges to store more blood
The ventricle wall thickens because it must generate higher pressures to force blood through the narrowed aortic valve
The coronary arteries expand to supply more blood
The ventricle thins out and dilates to increase volume

quiz_questions.explanation: With aortic stenosis, the left ventricle faces increased afterload because it must push blood through a narrowed valve; like any muscle that works against increased resistance, the ventricular wall undergoes concentric hypertrophy to generate the higher pressures needed.

quiz_questions.question_text: A 45-year-old patient has a total cholesterol of 280 mg/dL, LDL of 190 mg/dL, and HDL of 32 mg/dL. The physician prescribes a statin and lifestyle changes. Why is this lipid profile so concerning?

options.text: The total cholesterol is normal but HDL is too high
Very high LDL accelerates plaque formation while very low HDL means insufficient cholesterol removal from arteries, dramatically increasing CAD risk
These values are ideal and no treatment is needed
Only the total cholesterol matters, and 280 is borderline normal

quiz_questions.explanation: This patient has a highly atherogenic lipid profile: elevated LDL deposits excess cholesterol in arterial walls while low HDL provides inadequate reverse cholesterol transport; together, they dramatically accelerate atherosclerosis and increase the risk of MI and stroke.

quiz_questions.question_text: A 50-year-old man undergoes cardiac catheterization revealing a 95% blockage of his left main coronary artery. The cardiologist recommends CABG rather than a stent. Why would surgery be preferred over stenting for this particular blockage?

options.text: Stenting is never effective for coronary artery blockages
Left main coronary artery blockage is particularly dangerous because it supplies most of the left ventricle; CABG provides more durable and complete revascularization for this high-risk location
CABG is always less invasive than stenting
Stents cannot be placed in arteries larger than 3 mm

quiz_questions.explanation: Left main coronary artery disease is considered the most critical location because it supplies the majority of the left ventricle; CABG is often preferred because it provides multiple bypass routes and has been shown to have better long-term outcomes than stenting for this severe, high-risk anatomy.

quiz_questions.question_text: An ER physician tells a heart failure patient that their legs are swollen because of right-sided heart failure. The patient asks why a weak right ventricle causes leg swelling rather than breathing problems. What is the correct explanation?

options.text: Right-sided failure causes fluid to leak into the lungs

When the right ventricle cannot pump blood forward effectively, blood backs up into the systemic venous circulation, increasing venous pressure and causing fluid to leak into tissues, especially the legs
Right-sided failure only affects the brain, causing confusion
Leg swelling is caused by blocked arteries, not heart failure

quiz_questions.explanation: In right-sided heart failure, the right ventricle cannot adequately pump blood to the lungs, so blood backs up into the systemic venous system; increased venous hydrostatic pressure forces fluid out of capillaries into the interstitial spaces, causing dependent edema in the legs, ankles, and abdomen.

quiz_questions.question_text: A 28-year-old woman presents with a new heart murmur after a recent strep throat infection. The physician suspects rheumatic heart disease. How does a strep infection lead to valve damage?

options.text: Strep bacteria directly infect and destroy the heart valves
Antibodies against strep bacteria cross-react with heart valve tissue (molecular mimicry), causing autoimmune-mediated valve inflammation and damage
Strep infection causes blood clots that block the coronary arteries
The infection causes hypertension that damages the valves mechanically

quiz_questions.explanation: Rheumatic heart disease occurs when antibodies produced against group A streptococcus antigens cross-react with similar proteins in heart valve tissue (molecular mimicry), triggering an autoimmune inflammatory response that damages and scars the valves, most commonly the mitral valve.

quiz_questions.question_text: A patient is diagnosed with a massive saddle pulmonary embolism after developing sudden severe shortness of breath and chest pain following a long flight. The physician explains this is a cardiovascular emergency. Why is a large pulmonary embolism life-threatening?

options.text: It blocks blood flow to the brain, causing a stroke
It blocks blood flow through the pulmonary arteries, preventing gas exchange and causing right heart failure from acute pressure overload
It causes the aorta to rupture
It only causes mild coughing that resolves on its own

quiz_questions.explanation: A massive pulmonary embolism obstructs blood flow through the pulmonary arteries, dramatically increasing pulmonary vascular resistance; the right ventricle acutely fails under this sudden pressure overload, cardiac output plummets, and without emergency treatment the patient can die within minutes.

quiz_questions.question_text: A patient's cardiologist explains that prevention of cardiovascular disease should start early in life. A 35-year-old patient with no current symptoms asks what specific lifestyle changes provide the greatest benefit. Which combination is most evidence-based?

options.text: Only taking supplements and vitamins
Regular exercise, healthy diet, not smoking, maintaining healthy weight, and managing blood pressure and cholesterol
Only avoiding stress completely
Only having annual physical exams without lifestyle changes

quiz_questions.explanation: The strongest evidence for cardiovascular disease prevention supports a comprehensive approach: 150+ minutes per week of moderate exercise, a diet rich in fruits, vegetables, and whole grains, smoking cessation, maintaining a healthy BMI, and controlling BP and cholesterol to optimal levels.

quiz_questions.question_text: A 55-year-old patient with a mechanical aortic valve replacement must take warfarin (a blood thinner) for the rest of their life. The patient asks why this is necessary. What is the primary reason?

options.text: Warfarin prevents the mechanical valve from calcifying
Mechanical valve surfaces are thrombogenic, so anticoagulation is required to prevent blood clots from forming on the valve that could cause stroke or valve malfunction
Warfarin helps the body accept the foreign valve material
Warfarin reduces the risk of infection on the valve

quiz_questions.explanation: Mechanical heart valves have artificial surfaces that activate the clotting cascade when blood contacts them; without lifelong anticoagulation, thrombi can form on the valve, potentially causing valve obstruction, systemic embolization, or stroke.
